meta.title: DWP 2024 Midterm
meta.description: Midterm exam covering HTTP, HTML forms, DOM manipulation, events, and cookies.

questions.questionMd: Which HTTP method is **idempotent** and used to update an existing resource by replacing it entirely?
questions.explanationMd:
**PUT** is idempotent — sending the same PUT request multiple times has the same effect as sending it once. It replaces the entire resource at the target URI.

- **POST** is not idempotent (creates a new resource each time)
- **PATCH** is used for partial updates and not guaranteed to be idempotent
- **DELETE** is idempotent but used for removal, not updating

questions.questionMd: What does an HTTP **304 Not Modified** response indicate?

options.label: A
options.text: The request was successful and the resource is returned

options.label: B
options.text: The resource has not changed since the last request, so the browser can use its cached version

options.label: C
options.text: The server is redirecting to a different URL

options.label: D
options.text: The request is malformed and cannot be processed

questions.explanationMd:
A **304 Not Modified** response tells the client that the cached version of the resource is still valid. The server does not resend the resource body, saving bandwidth.

This is part of HTTP's **conditional request** mechanism using headers like `If-Modified-Since` and `ETag`/`If-None-Match`.

questions.questionMd: What is the default `method` and `enctype` used when submitting an HTML form without explicitly setting them?
questions.explanationMd:
By default, HTML forms use:
- **`method="GET"`** — data is appended to the URL as query parameters
- **`enctype="application/x-www-form-urlencoded"`** — data is encoded as key=value pairs separated by `&`

You must explicitly set `method="POST"` if you want to send data in the request body. For file uploads, you need `enctype="multipart/form-data"`.

questions.questionMd: Which HTML input attribute prevents a form from being submitted if the field is left empty?
questions.explanationMd:
The **`required`** attribute is a boolean attribute that tells the browser to block form submission if the field is empty.

- `placeholder` only shows hint text inside the input
- `autofocus` moves focus to the input on page load
- `novalidate` (on the `<form>` element) disables browser validation entirely

`querySelectorAll()` returns a **static NodeList** — it's a snapshot of matching elements at the time of the call. It does not update when the DOM changes.

Contrast with `getElementsByClassName()` which returns a **live HTMLCollection** that automatically reflects DOM changes.

Note: A NodeList is **not** a true Array, but you can convert it with `Array.from()` or the spread operator `[...nodeList]`.

questions.questionMd: What is the difference between `innerHTML` and `textContent` when setting content on an element?

options.label: A
options.text: There is no difference — they are aliases

options.label: B
options.text: `innerHTML` parses HTML tags; `textContent` treats everything as plain text

options.label: C
options.text: `textContent` parses HTML tags; `innerHTML` treats everything as plain text

questions.explanationMd:
**`innerHTML`** parses the string as HTML markup and renders it — this means HTML tags like `<b>`, `<a>`, etc. will be interpreted.

**`textContent`** treats the string as raw text — HTML tags will be displayed literally, not rendered.

⚠️ Using `innerHTML` with untrusted user input can lead to **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** attacks. Always sanitize or prefer `textContent` when you don't need HTML rendering.

questions.questionMd:
Given the following HTML, if the user clicks on the ``, in which order will the event handlers fire? ```html
``` Assuming all three elements have click event listeners attached.
questions.explanationMd:
Events in the DOM follow the **bubbling** phase by default. When a click occurs on the `<span>`:

1. The event fires on the **target** element (`span`) first
2. Then **bubbles up** to `button`
3. Then to `div`

This is the **bubbling order**: target → parent → grandparent → ... → document → window

You can stop this with `event.stopPropagation()`.

Note: The **capturing** phase (opposite order) can be used by passing `{ capture: true }` to `addEventListener`.

questions.questionMd: What is **event delegation** and why is it useful?

options.label: A
options.text: Attaching an event listener to every child element individually

options.label: B
options.text: Attaching a single event listener to a parent element and using `event.target` to determine which child was clicked

options.label: D
options.text: Preventing default browser behavior for all events

questions.questionMd: `event.preventDefault()` stops the event from bubbling up to parent elements.
questions.explanationMd:
**False.** `event.preventDefault()` prevents the **default browser action** (e.g., following a link, submitting a form) but does **not** stop event propagation/bubbling.

To stop bubbling, use `event.stopPropagation()`.

They serve different purposes:
- `preventDefault()` → stop the browser's default behavior
- `stopPropagation()` → stop the event from reaching other listeners

questions.questionMd: Which cookie attribute prevents client-side JavaScript from accessing the cookie via `document.cookie`?
questions.explanationMd:
The **`HttpOnly`** flag makes the cookie inaccessible to JavaScript's `document.cookie` API. The cookie is only sent in HTTP requests.

This is a critical security measure against **XSS attacks** — even if an attacker injects JavaScript, they cannot steal `HttpOnly` cookies.

- `Secure` — only sent over HTTPS
- `SameSite` — controls cross-origin request behavior (CSRF protection)
- `Path` — limits the cookie to a specific URL path

questions.questionMd: How does a server typically maintain a session with a client using cookies?

options.label: A
options.text: By storing all user data directly in the cookie

options.label: B
options.text: By sending a unique session ID in a cookie, and storing session data on the server

options.label: C
options.text: By using localStorage on the client

options.label: D
options.text: By embedding the session in the URL path

questions.explanationMd:
The standard approach is:
1. Server creates a **session ID** (a random, unique token)
2. Sends it to the client as a **cookie** (usually `HttpOnly` and `Secure`)
3. Client sends this cookie on every subsequent request
4. Server looks up the session data using the ID

This way, sensitive data stays on the **server**, and only a small identifier travels in the cookie.

The session ID must be:
- Cryptographically random
- Sufficiently long
- Regenerated on authentication changes to prevent session fixation

questions.questionMd: Which of the following are **request** headers (sent by the client)? Select all that apply.
questions.explanationMd:
**Request headers** (client → server):
- **`Accept`** — tells the server what content types the client can handle
- **`Authorization`** — sends credentials (Bearer tokens, Basic auth)
- **`Content-Type`** — describes the format of the request body

**Response headers** (server → client):
- **`Set-Cookie`** — instructs the browser to store a cookie
- **`X-Powered-By`** — reveals the server technology (should be disabled in production)

Note: `Content-Type` can appear in both requests and responses, but it is a **request header** when the client sends a body.

questions.questionMd: What is the difference between `childNodes` and `children` on a DOM element?

options.label: B
options.text: `childNodes` includes all node types (text, comments, elements); `children` includes only element nodes

options.label: C
options.text: `children` includes text nodes; `childNodes` only includes elements

options.label: D
options.text: `childNodes` is a method; `children` is a property

questions.explanationMd:
**`childNodes`** returns a NodeList of **all** child nodes, including: - Element nodes (`
`, `

`, etc.) - Text nodes (whitespace, text content) - Comment nodes (``) **`children`** returns an HTMLCollection of **only element** nodes. In most cases, you want `children` because whitespace text nodes between elements can cause unexpected results with `childNodes`.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly uses the `pattern` attribute to allow only lowercase letters and numbers (3–8 characters)?
questions.explanationMd:
The `pattern` attribute uses a **regex** that is automatically anchored — the browser wraps it in `^(?:...)$` internally.

So:
- `[a-z0-9]{3,8}` matches the entire value against 3–8 lowercase alphanumeric characters ✅
- `[a-z0-9]+` would allow any length ≥ 1
- `^[a-z0-9]{3,8}$` — the explicit anchors are redundant but not wrong in most browsers
- `/[a-z0-9]{3,8}/` — regex delimiters (`/`) are **not** used in the `pattern` attribute

questions.questionMd: HTTPS encrypts the URL path and query parameters in transit, but the domain name is always visible in the initial TLS handshake (via SNI).
questions.explanationMd:
**True.** HTTPS (TLS) encrypts the entire HTTP request including the path, query parameters, headers, and body. However, during the **TLS handshake**, the client sends the **Server Name Indication (SNI)** in plaintext, which reveals the domain name.

This means:
- ✅ `/api/users?id=123` is encrypted
- ❌ `example.com` is visible to network observers via SNI

Newer protocols like **ECH (Encrypted Client Hello)** aim to encrypt SNI as well.
